body:
Prerequisites Before you begin, ensure you have the following installed:
Python 3.9 or newer: Follow the official installation guide. PIP: If you haven't installed PIP yet, see the installation instructions. Getting Started Follow these steps to set up your environment and install the required package.
1. Setting Up a Virtual Environment Creating a virtual environment helps isolate your project dependencies. Use the command below to create a virtual environment named .venv:
1python3 -m venv .venv Works for Windows, Linux or macOS
2. Activating the Virtual Environment Activate your virtual environment with the following commands:
For Windows 1.venv\Scripts\activate For macOS and Linux 1source .venv/bin/activate 3. Installing the Package With your virtual environment activated, install the re-mocd package:
1pip install re-mocd Next Steps Discover the algorithms designed for various network environments. Depending on your network configuration, some may perform better than others. The image below offers a simplified visual comparison to help you choose the optimal solution.
title: Getting Started

body:
Introduction This tutorial demonstrates how to generate and visualize a graph with community structures detected using the NSGA-II algorithm. We will:
Generate a synthetic graph using the LFR benchmark model. Detect communities using NSGA-II-based detection from re_mocd. Use NetworkX and Seaborn to visualize the communities with distinct colors. Prerequisites Ensure you have the required libraries installed:
1pip install networkx matplotlib seaborn re_mocd Step 1: Generate a Graph with Community Structure We use the LFR benchmark graph to create a synthetic network with realistic community structures.
1import networkx as nx 2import matplotlib.pyplot as plt 3import seaborn as sns 4import re_mocd 5from networkx.generators.community import LFR_benchmark_graph 6 7# Set a clean Seaborn theme 8sns.set_theme(style="white") 9 10# LFR benchmark parameters 11n = 100 # Number of nodes 12tau1 = 3 # Degree distribution exponent 13tau2 = 1.5 # Community size distribution exponent 14mu = 0.1 # Mixing parameter (controls inter-community edges) 15 16# Generate the LFR benchmark graph 17G = LFR_benchmark_graph(n, tau1, tau2, mu, average_degree=5, min_community=30) Step 2: Detect Communities 1# Run the NSGA-II community detection algorithm 2communities = re_mocd.nsga_ii(G) Step 3: Visualize the Detected Communities To enhance visualization, we assign distinct colors to each detected community and display the graph.
1# Get unique community IDs 2unique_comms = set(communities.values()) 3 4# Generate a color palette for communities 5palette = sns.color_palette("hsv", len(unique_comms)) 6comm_color_map = {comm: palette[i] for i, comm in enumerate(unique_comms)} 7 8# Assign colors to nodes 9node_colors = [comm_color_map[communities[node]] for node in G.nodes()] Plot using matplotlib.pyplot:
pos = nx.spring_layout(G, seed=42) plt.figure(figsize=(16, 12)) nx.draw_networkx_edges(G, pos, edge_color='gray', alpha=0.7, width=2) nx.draw_networkx_nodes(G, pos, node_color=node_colors, edgecolors='black', linewidths=1.5, node_size=600) plt.axis('off') plt.show() Output ... ...
title: Visualizing Communities Detected

title: Examples

body:
Introduction In this guide, we'll explore how to use the nsga_ii function for community detection on a NetworkX graph. The NSGA-II algorithm employs a multi-objective approach to identify community structures within networks.
Prerequisites Ensure that you have installed the necessary libraries and the module:
1pip list | grep re-mocd If you're using a unix-based system, you should see:
re_mocd 0.2.3
Step 1: Prepare Your Graph We will use a sample graph for demonstration purposes. Here, we use the Karate Club graph:
1import networkx as nx 2 3# Create a sample graph 4G = nx.karate_club_graph() Step 2: Run the nsga_ii Algorithm Import and run the nsga_ii function on the graph, optionally specifying a debug level:
1from re_mocd import nsga_ii 2 3# Run the algorithm with a debug level of 1. 0 is the default. 4communities = nsga_ii(G, debug=1) Step 3: Inspect the Results The output is a dictionary mapping node IDs to community IDs. You can print the results as follows:
1print("Detected communities:") 2for node, comm in communities.items(): 3 print(f"Node {node}: Community {comm}")
title: Non-Dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm 2

body:
Introduction This tutorial demonstrates how to use both pesa_ii_minimax and pesa_ii_maxq functions to detect communities in a NetworkX graph. Both algorithms take a graph as input and return a mapping of node IDs to their detected community IDs. The debug parameter is available to control the verbosity of the output.
Prerequisites Before you begin, ensure you have installed the required libraries and the module containing these algorithms:
1pip install your_module_name Step 1: Prepare Your Graph For demonstration purposes, we use the classic Karate Club graph from NetworkX:
1import networkx as nx 2 3# Create a sample graph (Karate Club graph) 4G = nx.karate_club_graph() Step 2: Running pesa_ii_minimax The pesa_ii_minimax function performs community detection using a minimax approach. Import the function from your module and run it on your graph:
1from your_module_name import pesa_ii_minimax 2 3# Run the pesa_ii_minimax algorithm with a debug level of 2 4communities_minimax = pesa_ii_minimax(G, debug=2) 5 6print("Detected communities (minimax):") 7for node, comm in communities_minimax.items(): 8 print(f"Node {node}: Community {comm}") Step 3: Running pesa_ii_maxq Similarly, the pesa_ii_maxq function applies a maxq approach to community detection. Use the following code to run it:
1from your_module_name import pesa_ii_maxq 2 3# Run the pesa_ii_maxq algorithm with a debug level of 2 4communities_maxq = pesa_ii_maxq(G, debug=2) 5 6print("Detected communities (maxq):") 7for node, comm in communities_maxq.items(): 8 print(f"Node {node}: Community {comm}")
title: Pareto Envelope-Based Selection Algorithm 2

body:
Introduction This tutorial demonstrates how to get the score of a graph given a community partition using the fitness function. This score measures the quality of the community structure.
Step 1: Prepare Your Graph and Partition Start by generating a sample graph and creating a partition. In this example, we use the Karate Club graph and define a simple partition:
1import networkx as nx 2 3# Create a sample graph 4G = nx.karate_club_graph() 5 6# Example partition: mapping node IDs to community IDs (this is just a simple example) 7partition = {node: 0 if node < 10 else 1 for node in G.nodes()} Step 2: Calculate the Modularity Score Import the fitness function from your module and compute the modularity score:
1from re_mocd import fitness 2 3# Calculate the modularity score for the graph and partition 4modularity_score = fitness(G, partition) 5print("Score:", modularity_score) Using the fitness function, you can evaluate the quality of a community partition by calculating its score. Experiment with different partitions based on your community detection results for further analysis.
title: Checking fitness of a partition

body:
Python Multiobjective Evolutionary Algorithms pyevoea is a Python lib for efficient multi-objective evolutionary algorithms in community detection. It enhances performance for large-scale graph analysis. This project continues from re-mocd.
Have ideas for improvement? Open a PR or report an issue to help us evolve re-mocd further. Built for 100% compatibility with networkx, our library integrates seamlessly into your projects. (Support for weighted graphs is on the roadmap!).
Designed to handle large-scale networks efficiently and fast, while simultaneously optimizes multiple metrics.
Read the Docs View on PyPI
Example output from an LFR benchmark graph made in 0.42ms.
title: re-mocd
